DEMURRER to COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Sep-24-2021 Off Calendar
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT SAN FRANCISCO MARIN MEDICAL SOCIETY'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT.

No appearances are made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant San Francisco Marin Medical Society's demurrer is ordered off calendar for noncompliance with CCP 430.41, which requires a "meet and confer in person or by telephone" before a demurrer is filed. The parties are ordered to comply with the code and immediately meet and confer. Defendant San Francisco Marin Medical Society's response to the complaint is now due by October 22, 2021.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, Email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/RBU)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Aug-06-2021 Continued To Oct-13-21 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER to COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jul-29-2021 Overruled
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. BAHAREH HABIBI, Esq. of Wood, Smith, Henning & Berman LLP (Tel: 925-222-3400) for defendant Mary Lou Licwinko;
No other appearances made in this matter.

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT MARY LICWINKO'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT.

Matter not contested/argued. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Ms. Licwinko's demurrer is overruled in its entirety.
Plaintiff adequately pleads the elements of the fifth and sixth causes of action for fraud. "Promissory fraud is a subspecies of the action for fraud and deceit. A promise to do something necessarily implies the intention to perform; hence, where a promise is made without such intention, there is an implied misrepresentation of fact that may be actionable fraud. The elements of promissory fraud . . . are: (1) a promise made regarding a material fact without any intention of performing it; (2) the existence of the intent not to perform at the time the promise was made; (3) intent to deceive or induce the promisee to enter into a transaction; (4) reasonable reliance by the promisee; (5) nonperformance by the party making the promise; and (6) resulting damage to the promise." (Behnke v. State Farm General Ins. Co. (2011) 196 Cal.App.4th 1443, 1453.) Defendant's arguments that misrepresentation of a material fact, intent to deceive, and reasonable reliance are insufficiently pled are rejected. The complaint sufficiently alleges these elements. (See Compl. 12, 65, 70, 72, 77.) It also sufficiently alleges how, when, where, to whom, and by what means the representations were made. (Id.) The Court rejects Defendant's argument that the fifth and sixth causes of action are based on non-actionable opinions. Defendant's argument that Plaintiff cannot show reasonable reliance because her employment was at-will raises a factual issue outside the pleadings, and in any event will not be considered because Defendant first raised the argument in its reply. (See Hernandez v. First Student, Inc. (2019) 37 Cal.App.4th 270, 277 ["[w]e will not ordinarily consider issues raised for the first time in a reply brief."]; American Drug Stores, Inc. v. Stroh (1992) 10 Cal.App.4th 1446, 1453 ["Points raised for the first time in a reply brief will ordinarily not be considered, because such consideration would deprive the respondent of an opportunity to counter the argument."].)

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Rose Miller, CSR No. 5041, Email: RMillerCSR@gmail.com. (302/EPS)
